2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation Tianmeng Zhang a, Zhongyang Huang a, Dengshuo Cui a, Zicheng Luo a, Xiuping Zhuo a, Yanfeng Liu a, Xiuyan Wang a, Kai Wu a, Jianfeng Zhang b,*, Guibin Li c, Shumei Sun d,**, Han Zuilhof e,f,*** , Hao Lu a,**** a College of Materials and Textile Engineering, G60 STI Valley Industry & Innovation Institute, Jiaxing University, Jiaxing, 314001, Zhejiang, Chinab College of Materials Science and Engineering, Hohai University, Nanjing, 210098, Chinac College of Mechanical Engineering, Jiaxing University, Jiaxing, 314001, Chinad Department of Physics and Applied Optics, Beijing Area Major Laboratory, Beijing Normal University, Beijing, 100875, Chinae College of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Jiaxing University, Jiaxing, 314001, Chinaf Laboratory of Organic Chemistry, Wageningen University, Wageningen, 6708 WE, the Netherlands

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Defect engineeringTwo-dimensional nanosheetsFluid channelsDye/salt separationHydrogen bonded water at interface

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: A B S T R A C T Two-dimensional (2D) lamellar membranes hold immense promise for sustainable separation technologies, yet their inherent dense interlayer architecture compromises permeability and selectivity. Herein, we propose a defect engineering strategy to construct hierarchical nanofluidic channels within graphene oxide (GO) mem­branes, by using intercalating polypyrrole (PPy) nanosheets with in-plane nanopores. The synergistic assembly of GO and PPy nanosheets enables tuning of both interlayer spacing and intra-plane nanopores, thereby generating multiscale transport pathways. The optimized PPy/GO membrane achieves a trade-off between selectivity and permeability: >96 % rejection for Congo red, <15 % rejection for salts (NaCl, Na2SO4, CaCl2), and a remarkable water flux of 310 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 — 5-fold higher than state-of-the-art 2D membranes. Interface selective sum frequency generation (SFG) spectroscopy results reveal that PPy intercalation greatly enhances the order of hydrogen-bonded water at the membrane-water interface, which correlates with the fast and selective separation of dye and salt. Molecular dynamics and hydrodynamic simulations consolidate that the hierarchical channels enhance water permeation via defect-pore sieving, while selectively block organic dyes through size exclusion.

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: solidate that the hierarchical channels enhance water permeation via defect-pore sieving, while selectively block organic dyes through size exclusion. Our work not only disentangles the intricate role of defect engineering in tailoring organic/salt separation for 2D membranes, but also provides a scalable approach for advanced separation systems in hypersaline wastewater treatment and resource recovery.

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content:
1. Introduction In recent years, enormous efforts have been made to develop mem­brane materials for efficient, and preferably simultaneous, separation of both organics and salts from water [1–3], since such separation pro­cesses are of essential interest in the purification of organic molecules, refinement of inorganic salt ions, as well as water purification. Two-dimensional (2D) lamellar membranes show great potential in se­lective separations due to their interlayer channels and ultrathin thickness [4,5]. Graphene oxide (GO) [6], graphitic phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [7], MXene [8] and transition metal sulfide nanosheets [9] are promising candidates for assembly of 2D lamellar membranes. Among these 2D materials, GO sheets are known for their atomic-level thickness and ideal mechanical strength. However, their large lateral dimensions and dense interlayer geometry increase the path of water transport and resistance of mass transfer. The resulting low permeation flux and insufficient separation performance hamper further developments of GO laminar membranes towards large-scale application [10].

* Corresponding authors.** Corresponding author.*** Corresponding authors. citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: Defect engineering is an ideal bottom-up strategy to achieve profit­able structural defects during synthesizing 2D nanosheets. Such strategy opens up an effective and facile avenue in fabricating 2D lamellar membranes by constructing the ordered channels for free mass transfer, therefore, defect engineering has been widely exploited in multiple applications to yield extraordinary improvements in adsorption, sepa­ration and catalytic performances [11,12]. To create the defects, hole creation on GO nanosheets using top-down methods, e.g., template-orientated, physical and chemical etching, have been applied [13–15]. These methods greatly improve water transport and selective separation. However, obtaining 2D lamellar membranes with high water flux, low salt rejection, and high organic rejection still face challenges, such as harsh etching methods for pore formation, as well as lack of control over interlayer channels. Regarding the interlayer channels, interlayer interpolation offers a promising approach for regulating the interlayer distance. Specific substances (e.g., metal ions, polymers, MOFs, nanoparticles, etc.) are inserted into the layers of GO lamellar membranes [16–18], via which the interlay channels of 2D GO can be effectively regulated. Xu et al. [19] established nanofluidic heterostructures channels by mixing g-C3N4 and GO nanosheets, which dramatically improved the desali­nation rate and water permeability. Guo et al.

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: ic heterostructures channels by mixing g-C3N4 and GO nanosheets, which dramatically improved the desali­nation rate and water permeability. Guo et al. [20] mix boron nitride (BN) and GO nanosheets, the resulting GO/BN hybrid membranes, as featured with nano-channels, greatly improves efficiency for purifica­tion and separation of the solutes. Apparently, combining GO and other type of nanosheets is suitable approach for constructing membranes with fluid channels, which benefits for improved water permeability and organic/salt separation. Herein, we explore finetuned defect engineering coupled with an insertion assembly strategy for the fabrication of 2D lamellar composite membranes. Specifically, we fabricated polypyrrole (PPy) nanosheets enriched with nanopores using a bottom-up synthesis method, which, were synergistically self-assembled with GO to form multilayered PPy/ GO membranes. The composite lamellar membranes are designed with tunable interlayer channels and intralayer nanopores. Thanks to the construction of this system, the PPy/GO composite membrane exhibited excellent separation performance, achieving >96 % rejection of organic dyes, <15 % rejection of inorganic salts, and in particular the permeate flux as high as 5 times of reported values.

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: ieving >96 % rejection of organic dyes, <15 % rejection of inorganic salts, and in particular the permeate flux as high as 5 times of reported values. We systematically investigate the membrane-water interface by combining surface-specific sum fre­quency generation spectroscopy, molecular dynamics simulations, and hydrodynamics simulations, our results reveal the channeled membrane structure and associated ordered interfacial water attribute for the fast and selective dye/salt separation. 2. Experimental 2.1. Chemicals and materials Block copolymer (PS-b-PEO) was purchased from Ruixi Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Xi’an), GO was purchased from the Sixth Element materials technology Co. (Changzhou), Ltd. Sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), congo red (CR) were provided by Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd. Pyrrole (Py), octa­decylamine (ODA), tetrahydrofuran (THF), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ammonium persulfate (APS) were supplied by Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, Φ50 mm, 0.22 μm) was purchased from Tianjin Jinteng Experiment Equipment Co., Ltd. (Tianjin). Ultra­pure water was used for all experiments. All reagents were of analytical grade unless otherwise noted. 2.2. Fabrication of PPy nanosheet with nanopores 0.05 g of PS-b-PEO (Polystyrene-block-poly(ethylene oxide)) was dissolved in 9 mL of the precast solution (VTHF:VH2O = 1:8) to form micellar aggregates.

content: 05 g of PS-b-PEO (Polystyrene-block-poly(ethylene oxide)) was dissolved in 9 mL of the precast solution (VTHF:VH2O = 1:8) to form micellar aggregates. Subsequently, 2 g of ODA (2 wt%) solution in ethanol was added to the above solution, which generates the target template. Afterwards, 30 μL of Py, 0.3 mL of NaOH (1 mol/L), and APS

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: (0.18 g) were added sequentially, and the polymerization reaction was carried out under continuous stirring. The 2D PPy nanosheets were isolated by high-speed centrifugation, and washed repeatedly with THF, ethanol and water to remove templates and excess ions. The final PPy nanosheets with nanopores were obtained by freeze-drying. 2.3. Fabrication of PPy/GO composite membrane The PPy/GO membranes were fabricated by vacuum-assisted filtra­tion. Firstly, 0.02 mg/ml of GO solution and 0.5 mg/mL of PPy nano­sheet solution were prepared using ultrasonic dispersion. Different proportions of GO and PPy solutions were mixed to obtain the disper­sions, and different PPy/GO composite membranes were synthesized onto polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) substrates. The composite membranes (mass density of 112 mg/m2) were named PPy/GO-x, and x corresponded to the mass ratio of PPy to GO, which was 2, 2.5, 3.3, 5 and 10, respectively. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Fabrication and characterization of PPy/GO membranes Fig. 1 illustrates the fabrication and microstructure of the PPy nanosheet with nanopores. As sketched in Fig. 1 (a), assembled amphiphilic octadecylamine (ODA) in ethanol/water solution provides a soft template for the growth of two-dimensional nanosheets [21]. The PS-b-PEO block copolymer self-assembles into spherical micelles in THF/H2O (1: 8) mixed solvent. Such polymeric micelles adsorb on the

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: Fig. 3. Surface properties of GO and PPy/GO membranes. (a) FTIR spectra of GO and PPy/GO membranes. (b) Static water contact angles of GO and PPy/GO membranes. (c) Rejection measurements for PPy/GO membranes via PEGs with different molecular weight. (d) Pore size distribution of PPy/GO membranes as derived from the rejection rates in (c). T. Zhang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124602 stretching vibrations of pyrrole [24]. In-plane and out-of-plane bending vibrational peaks of C–H appear in the region of 1000–1200 cm-1 [25]. The surface wetting behavior of composite membranes was studied by water contact angle measurements, the data are presented in Fig. 3 (b). The static contact angles of PPy/GO composite membranes are ~90◦, suggesting they are relatively hydrophilic. As compared to pure GO, a slight elevation of water contact angle for composite membranes is possibly associated with their change of surface structure. Separation properties of PPy/GO membranes were firstly studied by quantifying their rejection percentage of model PEG polymers with varying molec­ular weight, as shown in Fig. 3 (c), these composite membranes show increasing rejection with increasing molecular weight. We further quantify the curves in Fig. 3 (c), a lognormal model of the pore radius distribution was created based on the probability density function be­tween the PEG cutoff curve and the Stokes radius [26] (Fig. 3 (d)).

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: pore radius distribution was created based on the probability density function be­tween the PEG cutoff curve and the Stokes radius [26] (Fig. 3 (d)). The average pore sizes for series of PPy/GO membranes were derived to be 1.36 nm, 1.42 nm, 1.44 nm, 1.68 nm, and 1.62 nm, respectively (inset of Fig. 3 (d)). The average pore size of PPy/GO composite membranes in­creases with increasing the content of PPy nanosheet, and the intro­duction of more PPy sheets effectively modulates both the distribution of inner layer nanopores and the inter layer spacing for the PPy/GO composite membranes. Apparently, average pore size of composite membranes can be well adjusted by varying the mixing ratio of PPy and GO.

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: 3.2. Dye/salt separation for PPy/GO membranes The dye/salt separation for PPy/GO membranes were examined by means of dead-end filtration. Fig. 4 (a) shows the photographs of the separation device, as well as the solution before and after separation. The orange-red Congo Red (CR) solution becomes transparent after filtration by PPy/GO membrane, which indicates capability of the membrane to separate organic dyes. Fig. 4 (b) compares separation properties for different PPy/GO membranes. With increasing the per­centage of PPy, i.e., from PPy/GO-2 to PPy/GO-10, the water perme­ability gradually increases, which is consistent with the increasing size of nanopores with the membranes (Fig. 3(c and d)). It is worth noting that the average pore size of the PPy/GO-10 membrane is slightly smaller than that of the PPy/GO-5 membrane, but the wider normal distribution of pore sizes renders it more likely to contain large pores, resulting in a lower CR rejection rate. Multiple filtration measurement on typical PPy/GO-3.3 membrane in Fig. 4 (c) suggests the rejection rate remained essentially unchanged, while the slight decrease in water flux could be attributed to the continuous deposition of dyes on the mem­brane surface. The stability of PPy/GO-3.3 membranes during separa­tion performance were substantiated by UV–vis measurements upon prolonged immersion in water (Fig. S8). The UV absorbance spectra in Fig. 4 (d) confirm that the CR was effectively filtered.

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: UV–vis measurements upon prolonged immersion in water (Fig. S8). The UV absorbance spectra in Fig. 4 (d) confirm that the CR was effectively filtered. To display the wide scope of these membranes, we have – in addition to CR – also tested five other dyes, which vary in e.g., polarity and molecular weight. To our delight, the PPy/GO-3.3 membranes also exhibit a satisfying rejec­tion capacity for all these other dyes, while we observe that the rejection rates show a positive correlation with respect to molecular weight. Detailed, data can be observed in the Supporting Information (Fig. S7). Fig. 4 (e) presents the water permeance as well as the rejection per­centages of varying salts for PPy/GO-3.3 membrane. (b) Permeance and rejection percentage of CR by using different PPy/GO membranes. (c) Normalized water flux and rejection percentage of CR by using PPy/GO-3.3 membrane, as measured during multiple filtrations. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124602 condition of high rejection (>95 %) of dyes, the rejection percentages for NaCl, Na2SO4 and CaCl2 almost remained <20 %. Next, we systematically evaluated the selective dyes/salt separation for PPy/GO and GO membranes. Fig. 5 (a) summarizes permeances and the ratios of separation percentage for CR and NaCl in terms of different membranes. Obviously, doping PPy nanosheets with defect pores sub­stantially enhance water permeance, which exhibits a nearly 100-fold higher water flux as compared to the pure GO membrane. In addition, PPy/GO composite membranes have capability for selective dye/salt separation – in contrast to the high rejection of both dyes and salts for GO membranes.

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: /GO composite membranes have capability for selective dye/salt separation – in contrast to the high rejection of both dyes and salts for GO membranes. This selective separation also applies for other dye/salt solutions, such as CR/Na2SO4 and CR/CaCl2 solutions (Fig. 5 (b)). Fig. 5 (c) compares the separation performance of PPy/GO membranes with reported values in previous literatures. The composite PPy/GO mem­branes show separation efficiency for dye and salt as comparable to literatures, while notably, the permeate flux enhances by more than five times (see Table S1 for details). In this context, this defect engineering strategy of synthesizing membranes shows great potential for applica­tion in the pretreatment of wastewater, which are over-level of inorganic salts and organic dyes. Having established that integrating PPy nanosheets with GO en­hances dye/salt separation, it is apparent that the interaction of defect nanopores and intercalation assembly plays a critical role for molecular/ ion transport. We, therefore, need to identify the water-salt-dye trans­port law in the PPy/GO composite. As shown in the inset of Fig. 5 (d), we control the assembled diaphragm in the middle of the H-shaped elec­trolytic cell. Fig. 5 (d) presents the variation of conductivity with increasing time, reflecting the diffusion of salt ions. The concentration of

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: CR was also monitored by UV–Vis’s spectroscopy. The dye concentra­tions are all close to zero, which suggests that both GO and PPy/GO membranes are effective to block dyes. Despite that the salt ions can all diffuse through lamellar membranes, leading to increase of conductiv­ity, the transport of salt ions differs among the membranes. For GO, the ion transport is not obvious, and at 40 min a large jump of ion transport occurred, after which the ion transport showed a linear growth. This process is mainly due to the swelling of the GO lamellar membrane, i.e., water and salt ions enter the GO lamellae to increase the lamellar spacing under water-hydrogen bonding and ionic intercalation. This diffusion process is promoted, as featured by the disappearing ‘jump’ curve, with increasing the content of PPy, e.g., at PPy/GO-3.3. The data above also demonstrate the positive effect of intercalating PPy nano­sheets on increasing salt ion diffusion channels. 3.3. Mechanism of separation and permeability enhancements Apparently, our data provide clear evidence that the PPy/GO membranes show enhanced water permeance and selective separation of dye/salt as compared to pure GO membrane. The membrane function is largely mediated by the structure of water at membrane surface, for this, we probe the interfacial water using surface specific SFG spectroscopy.

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: n is largely mediated by the structure of water at membrane surface, for this, we probe the interfacial water using surface specific SFG spectroscopy. In this method, high power visible and infrared laser pulses are over­lapped at the interface both in time and space, generating photons at the sum frequency of the two beams. In this non-linear optical spectroscopic approach, the SFG signal is enhanced when the frequency of the IR pulse is resonant with the vibrational modes of the interfacial molecules. The selection rules dictate that the SFG signal is only generated from ordered

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: Fig. 6. Structure of interfacial water for PPy/GO membranes as probed by SFG spectroscopy. (a) Sketch of SFG measurement to probe interfaces between membrane and aqueous solution. (b) SFG spectra in CH/OH region for GO and different PPy/GO membranes. The spectra were recorded under SSP polarization (Spolarized SFG, S-polarized VIS, and P-polarized IR) combinations, and spectral fits (thinner lines) are superimposed. The spectra were offset for better comparison. (c) Fitting amplitudes of two OH bands at 3129 cm-1 and 3322 cm-1 for GO and PPy/GO membranes. T. Zhang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124602 contrasted in Fig. 7 (f) and (i), the PPy/GO layers have a significant enhancement of the permeation flow rate compared to the pure GO, as evidenced by the denser flow lines and more pronounced color change for the PPy/GO case. Therefore, the CFD analysis above consolidate again that the PPy/GO composite membranes form multiscale fluid channels with coexistence of intra-plane pores and interlayer channels, which leads to enhanced ion transport and increased water pathways. 4. Conclusions In this paper we demonstrate that the simple mixing of polypyrrole nanosheets with graphene oxide nanosheets can form in one step highly tunable and stable lamellar stacks which display tunable nanochannels.

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: lypyrrole nanosheets with graphene oxide nanosheets can form in one step highly tunable and stable lamellar stacks which display tunable nanochannels. This hierarchical nanoarchitecture is shown to be highly useful as a highly selective yet highly permeable membrane with dual-scale trans­port pathways via enlarged interlayer spacing and in-plane nanopores, as confirmed by molecular dynamics simulations. Specifically, in the case of membranes with a PPy/GO ratio of 3.3/1, we achieved >96 % rejection for organic dyes, <15 % salt permeation (NaCl, Na2SO4,

citation.title: 2D lamellar membranes with defect-engineered hierarchical channels impart ultrafast and selective dye/salt separation

content: CaCl2), and in particular water flux as high as of 310 L m-2 h-1 bar-1. Finally, our surface-specific SFG results reveal that PPy intercalation substantially enhances the order of strongly hydrogen-bonded water molecules at the membrane interface, which explains both the water permeation and selective dye/salt separation. We believe this trivially obtainable defect-engineering at the nanoscale deserves finite, delicate tuning in future work. This strategy displays large potential in the field of membrane science, and establishes a scalable defect-engineering approach to tailor nanofluidic architectures for both clean water sup­ply and resource recovery.
